purpose: Two standalone bash validator scripts — `validate-skill.sh` and `validate-subagent.sh` — that check the structural and content quality of Claude Code skills and subagent markdown files generated in Phase 4. They run automatically in Phase 5 (`lib/validate.sh`) and are also exercised directly by the bats test suite in `tests/scripts/`.

architecture.overview: Each script is a single-file, self-contained linter that accepts one file path as its sole argument, runs a series of ordered checks (structural → frontmatter fields → content quality → summary), accumulates ERRORS and WARNINGS counts, emits a human-readable verdict, and exits with code 0 (PASS/NEEDS REVISION) or 1 (FAIL). The two scripts share an identical internal skeleton: argument guard → file-exists check → structural checks → field checks → body/content checks → results summary → exit. There is no shared library between them; the duplication is intentional to keep each script fully self-contained.

subdirectories.purpose: Production validator scripts invoked by lib/validate.sh and directly exercised by tests/scripts/
bats-core test files that exercise validate-skill.sh and validate-subagent.sh in isolation, using a mix of checked-in fixtures and inline heredoc test cases
Checked-in SKILL.md fixtures for positive and negative test cases
Checked-in subagent fixtures for positive and negative test cases

architecture.data_flow: lib/validate.sh extracts each skill's content from synthesis/output.json via jq, writes it to WORK_DIR/validation/skills/<name>/SKILL.md, then calls `bash scripts/validate-skill.sh` on that temp file. The script writes all output to stdout/stderr, and validate.sh captures the combined output with 2>&1 to count ERROR:/WARNING: lines. Exit code 0 = pass (no errors, warnings within threshold); exit code 1 = fail (at least one ERROR). validate.sh aggregates failures, then triggers run_revision_agent() if any errors exist. The revision agent patches fixed artifacts back into output.json via jq in-place replacement.

key_files.purpose: Validates a SKILL.md file: checks filename=SKILL.md, folder is kebab-case, frontmatter delimiters exist, name/description fields present, description ≤1024 chars with trigger phrases and negative scope, no angle brackets, ≥3 codebase path references, verification section present, and generic phrase count ≤2. Errors → exit 1; >3 warnings → exit 0 with NEEDS REVISION; otherwise exit 0 PASS.

Validates a subagent .md file: checks .md extension, lowercase-hyphens filename, frontmatter delimiters, name/description fields, system prompt body ≥20 words, model field value (sonnet/opus/haiku/inherit), bypassPermissions warning, tool-scope coherence (reviewer without write tools), ≥3 codebase path references, and built-in Explore agent overlap. >4 warnings → NEEDS REVISION; errors → exit 1.

Full bats test coverage for validate-skill.sh: 14 test cases covering valid pass, invalid fixtures, inline heredoc edge cases (YAML folded/literal scalars, root-level file refs, tests/ refs, real angle brackets, nonexistent path, size limits).
bats test coverage for validate-subagent.sh: 5 tests covering valid pass, missing frontmatter, nonexistent file, missing name field, and uppercase filename warning.
Canonical reference fixture for a passing skill: demonstrates correct frontmatter (multi-line description with trigger + Do NOT use), ≥3 backtick-wrapped path refs, and a Verification section.
Canonical reference fixture for a passing subagent: shows name, description with triggers and negative scope, model field, allowedTools restriction, and ≥3 path references.

Phase 5 orchestrator that calls both scripts. Important for understanding how exit codes and ERROR:/WARNING: prefixes are consumed, how artifacts are written to temp paths before validation, and how run_revision_agent patches failures back.

patterns.name: Accumulated counter pattern
patterns.description: Both scripts use `ERRORS=0` and `WARNINGS=0` at the top, then `ERRORS=$((ERRORS + 1))` on each failure. All checks run unconditionally — no early exit on first error — so the full picture is always reported. The final block decides PASS/FAIL/NEEDS REVISION from the totals. This is intentional: synthesis agents need the full list of issues when revision is triggered.

patterns.name: Prefixed output protocol
patterns.description: All human-readable messages use prefixed lines: `ERROR:` goes to stderr (>&2) and `WARNING:` also to stderr, while informational lines (size, path refs) go to stdout. lib/validate.sh captures stdout+stderr together with 2>&1, then counts `^ERROR:` and `^WARNING:` lines using grep -c. This prefix convention is the coupling contract between the scripts and their caller.

patterns.name: awk two-pass frontmatter parser
patterns.description: Both scripts use an awk program to locate and extract the description field, including multi-line YAML folded (`>`) and literal (`|`) scalar blocks. Pattern: `NR==start` strips the field key, then `NR>start && /^  /` appends continuation lines, then `NR>start { exit }` stops at the next field. This handles the YAML multi-line case without a real YAML parser.

patterns.name: Warn-not-fail for soft quality issues
patterns.description: Items that indicate poor quality but aren't hard structural errors use WARNINGS rather than ERRORS: name/filename mismatch, missing trigger phrases, missing negative scope, generic phrase count, tool-scope incoherence, bypassPermissions. This lets the validator give a nuanced NEEDS REVISION verdict (>3 or >4 warnings) without hard-failing artifacts that are structurally sound.

patterns.name: Regex-based path reference counter
patterns.description: Both scripts count codebase-specific references using a single grep -cE pattern that matches backtick-wrapped filenames (with extension) and well-known directory prefixes (src/, lib/, tests/, scripts/, etc.). The threshold is ≥3 for ERRORS in skills, WARNING in subagents. The regex intentionally accepts common directory names without backticks to handle both inline code and list formats.

patterns.name: Heredoc inline test fixtures
patterns.description: validate_skill.bats creates many fixtures inline via heredoc `cat > "$TEST_TMPDIR/..."/SKILL.md <<'EOF'` rather than checking in files. This is used for edge cases (uppercase folder, no path refs, long description, generic phrases, missing verification) that are cheap to generate and would clutter the fixtures directory. Permanent positive/negative cases live as checked-in fixtures.

conventions.naming: Script filenames use kebab-case with .sh extension (validate-skill.sh, validate-subagent.sh). bats test files mirror the script name with underscores and .bats extension (validate_skill.bats). Fixture directories for skills use kebab-case folder names containing SKILL.md (matching the convention enforced by validate-skill.sh itself). Subagent fixtures are flat .md files named in kebab-case.
conventions.imports: No sourcing or imports. Each script is entirely self-contained. They depend only on: bash builtins (set, echo, [[ ]]), POSIX utilities (grep, sed, awk, wc, head, tail, basename, dirname). No jq, no lib/ sources. This independence is the key design constraint.
conventions.error_handling: set -euo pipefail at the top of each script. Individual check failures are caught with `|| true` to prevent pipeline exits from aborting the validator mid-run (e.g., `grep -c ... || true` when grep finds zero matches exits with code 1). Explicit `exit 1` at the end when ERRORS > 0.
other: Threshold asymmetry: validate-skill.sh fails on >3 warnings, validate-subagent.sh fails on >4 warnings — subagents have more optional checks (model, bypassPermissions, tool scope) so the bar is higher.
Error messages always use >&2 redirection and the prefix 'ERROR:' or 'WARNING:' so that lib/validate.sh can parse them with grep -c '^ERROR:'.
Token estimation: both scripts print a rough token estimate using the heuristic `WORD_COUNT * 13 / 10` (1.3 tokens per word).
FRONTMATTER_END is captured with awk before any field-level checks; all downstream checks that need body content guard with `if [ -n "$FRONTMATTER_END" ]` to avoid operating on an unparsed file.

internal: lib/validate.sh — calls both scripts; writes temp files before invocation; consumes exit codes and ERROR:/WARNING: prefix counts
tests/helpers/test_helper.bash — provides PROJECT_ROOT, TEST_TMPDIR, _common_setup/_common_teardown used by all .bats files in tests/scripts/
tests/fixtures/skills/ — checked-in SKILL.md fixtures consumed by validate_skill.bats
tests/fixtures/subagents/ — checked-in .md fixtures consumed by validate_subagent.bats

external_key.name: bash (≥3.2)
external_key.purpose: Script interpreter; uses arrays, [[ ]], arithmetic expansion, here-strings

external_key.name: grep
external_key.purpose: Pattern matching for frontmatter fields, path refs, generic phrases, trigger phrases — used extensively with -qE/-cE/-ciE flags

external_key.name: awk
external_key.purpose: Two-pass frontmatter delimiter detection (FRONTMATTER_END) and multi-line description extraction

external_key.name: sed
external_key.purpose: Strip field key prefixes from extracted values (name:, description:, model:)

external_key.name: wc
external_key.purpose: Line count, word count for size reporting and threshold checks

external_key.name: head
external_key.purpose: Read first line for frontmatter delimiter check; extract body lines after frontmatter

external_key.name: tail
external_key.purpose: Extract body content after frontmatter for subagent checks

external_key.name: bats-core + bats-assert + bats-support + bats-file
external_key.purpose: Test framework (Docker only); provides assert_success, assert_failure, assert_output --partial macros

gotchas.issue: validate-skill.sh derives the skill name from the *parent directory* (basename of dirname), not the file itself. A SKILL.md placed at any path will use its containing folder as the canonical name. If you put SKILL.md directly in a temp dir with an arbitrary name, the validator will check that folder name for kebab-case compliance and compare it against the `name:` frontmatter field.
gotchas.solution: Always create a subdirectory named after the skill before writing SKILL.md — exactly what lib/validate.sh does with `mkdir -p $WORK_DIR/validation/skills/$skill_name` and what tests do with `mkdir -p $TEST_TMPDIR/<skill-name>/`.

gotchas.issue: The description length check (≤1024 chars) applies to the *extracted and space-collapsed* description, not the raw lines. Multi-line YAML folded/literal scalars are assembled into a single space-joined string by the awk parser before the length check. A description that looks short line-by-line can still fail if the joined result is long.
gotchas.solution: For YAML folded descriptions, keep the total character count across all continuation lines under ~1024.

gotchas.issue: The path reference counter regex (`grep -cE '...'`) counts *lines* containing a match, not total occurrences. A single line with three backtick paths counts as 1. Meanwhile, `tests/` mentioned bare (without backticks) also counts if it matches the directory prefix pattern.
gotchas.solution: Spread references across at least 3 separate lines to ensure the line count reaches 3. The valid-skill fixture demonstrates this with one reference per bullet point line.

gotchas.issue: Warnings threshold differs between the two scripts: `validate-skill.sh` emits NEEDS REVISION at >3 warnings, `validate-subagent.sh` at >4 warnings. lib/validate.sh only counts ERROR: lines to decide whether to run_revision_agent — NEEDS REVISION has no effect on the revision trigger. NEEDS REVISION artifacts still get written.
gotchas.solution: NEEDS REVISION is a human-facing signal only. Don't rely on it as a programmatic gate — only ERRORS trigger automated revision.

gotchas.issue: The `grep -c` calls use `|| true` to suppress exit code 1 when grep finds zero matches. But if grep itself errors (bad regex, missing file), the `|| true` will swallow that too, leaving the counter at whatever partial value it had. This is particularly subtle in GENERIC_COUNT calculations.
gotchas.solution: This is accepted behavior for resilience. If you add new checks, follow the same `GENERIC_COUNT=$(grep ... || true); GENERIC_COUNT=${GENERIC_COUNT:-0}` pattern to handle both zero-match and error cases.

gotchas.issue: validate-subagent.sh checks tool-scope coherence only when `tools:` is on a single line (`grep -m1 '^tools:'`). Multi-line YAML tool lists (using `|` or array syntax) will cause TOOLS_VAL to be empty, triggering the 'no tools set' warning branch instead of the 'has Write/Edit' branch.
gotchas.solution: In generated subagent files, keep the tools: field as a comma-separated single-line value to get accurate coherence checking.

gotchas.issue: The revision agent in lib/validate.sh only fixes failed *skills* and CLAUDE.md generic phrases. Failed *subagents* are logged and counted toward the error total but are NOT fed to run_revision_agent. The revision prompt explicitly lists skills only.
gotchas.solution: If a subagent fails validation, it must be manually inspected. The pipeline will log the error but proceed to write without fixing it.

domain_terms.meaning: The canonical filename for a Claude Code skill file. Must always be named exactly SKILL.md, placed inside a kebab-case named directory. validate-skill.sh enforces both.
YAML block delimited by `---` at the start of both skill and subagent markdown files. Contains required fields: name, description. Optional: model, tools, allowedTools, permissionMode.
A phrase in the description that states when NOT to use the skill/agent (e.g., 'Do NOT use for frontend'). Enforced by regex matching `do not use|don't use|not for|instead use|do not trigger`.
A phrase in the description that states WHEN to invoke the skill/agent (e.g., 'Use when', 'Invoke for'). Checked by regex `use when|use for|trigger|invoke`.
The final one-line output of each validator: PASS (exit 0, few warnings), NEEDS REVISION (exit 0, many warnings), or FAIL (exit 1, errors present).

A backtick-wrapped filename with extension or a known directory prefix (src/, lib/, tests/, etc.) cited in the skill/agent body. Minimum 3 required. Signals the artifact is tailored to a specific project rather than generic.
A direct `claude -p` call in lib/validate.sh that receives failed skill content + issues and returns fixed versions. Only triggered for skills with ERROR-level failures.
A subagent frontmatter field that disables Claude Code's permission checks. validate-subagent.sh emits a WARNING when it detects this as it represents a security-sensitive configuration.

commands.purpose: Validate a single skill file directly; outputs verdict to stdout/stderr, exits 0 (pass) or 1 (fail)
Validate a single subagent file; same output protocol as validate-skill.sh
Run only the tests/scripts/*.bats suite inside Docker (validate_skill.bats + validate_subagent.bats)
Run the full bats test suite including scripts tests
Build the Docker test image (needed once before running any make test-* command)

skill_opportunities.name: validate-artifact
skill_opportunities.description: Run validate-skill.sh and/or validate-subagent.sh on a generated artifact, interpret the results, and if validation fails trigger targeted fixes following the same rules as run_revision_agent in lib/validate.sh.
workflow_steps: Determine artifact type (skill or subagent) from file path
Run the appropriate validator script and capture stdout+stderr
Parse ERROR:/WARNING: prefixed lines from output
If errors: build a fix prompt including the specific issues and original content
Apply targeted fixes and re-run validation to confirm PASS
